data.title: Telenor (OB:TEL) Margin Jump To 22% Tests Bearish Earnings Decline Narrative
data.description:
Advertisement

Telenor Q1 2026 earnings snapshot

Telenor (OB:TEL) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of NOK14.8b and basic EPS of NOK5.86, setting the tone against a trailing twelve month EPS of NOK12.29 on revenue of NOK76.4b. Over recent quarters the company has seen quarterly revenue range from NOK16.1b to NOK20.3b while basic EPS moved between NOK0.41 and NOK5.86. This gives investors a clear view of how earnings power has shifted relative to a fairly tight revenue band. With trailing net income excluding extra items at NOK16.8b and a net margin that has moved higher over the past year, the latest print points to a business where profitability rather than top line scale is doing most of the work.

See our full analysis for Telenor.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this margin focused story lines up with the most common narratives around Telenor's growth, risk profile, and income appeal.

See what the community is saying about Telenor

TTM earnings climb faster than revenue

  • Over the last 12 months, Telenor generated NOK76.4b in revenue and NOK16.8b in net income excluding extra items, which works out to a 22% net margin compared with 11.6% a year earlier and TTM earnings growth of 81% versus revenue growth of 1.3%.
  • Analysts' consensus view highlights gradual operating leverage from upselling and cost transformation, and the current numbers partly line up with that idea, but there is also some tension:
    • Consensus points to structurally lower operating expenses and better product mix in the Nordics helping earnings, and the move to a 22% net margin on roughly flat TTM revenue of about NOK76b illustrates that earnings per kroner of sales have become more efficient.
    • At the same time, consensus assumes revenue remains fairly flat over the next few years, so the modest 1.3% revenue growth alongside a large 81% earnings jump reminds you to check how much of that profit lift is tied to items that may not repeat in the same way.

Valuation, P/E gap and DCF fair value

  • At a share price of NOK152.5, Telenor trades on a trailing P/E of 12.4x, below the European telecom industry at 18.8x and a peer average of 27.6x, and is shown as trading below a DCF fair value of about NOK300.34.
  • Supporters of the bullish narrative often point to that valuation gap and dividend as key parts of the appeal, and the data gives some clear reference points:
    • The shares are flagged as trading about 49% below the DCF fair value of NOK300.34 while still offering a 6.36% trailing dividend yield, which is a combination bulls see as attractive when paired with the 22% net margin.
    • Consensus price targets in the data are centred on NOK171.16, which sits above the current NOK152.5 share price, so anyone reading the bullish case can compare that target, the DCF fair value and the current P/E discount to peers side by side.
On a day when Telenor reports a 22% net margin and trades on a 12.4x P/E, some investors want to see how the optimistic narrative ties those numbers to future earnings potential and capital allocation decisions before they make up their own mind about the stock. 🐂 Telenor Bull Case

Earnings forecasts versus high debt

  • The same dataset that shows TTM earnings up 81% and margins at 22% also shows forecasts for earnings to decline by about 4.9% per year over the next three years, with the company carrying a high level of debt.
  • Skeptics in the bearish narrative focus on whether these headwinds could limit the durability of recent profit strength, and the provided figures speak directly to that concern:
    • Bears argue that a forecast 4.9% yearly earnings decline sits awkwardly next to the recent 81% TTM earnings growth, and they point to the high debt level in the risk summary as a reason to question how flexible the balance sheet might be if conditions become tougher in key markets.
    • The risk section also flags elevated spectrum and network costs in markets like Bangladesh and pressure from 5G structures in Malaysia, so when you set those alongside forecasts for lower earnings, it helps explain why some investors concentrate less on the current 6.36% dividend yield and more on whether those cash flows can comfortably cover debt and required investment.

data.title: Capital Power Extends Arlington Valley Deal As New CFO Takes Over
data.description:
• Capital Power extended its Arlington Valley tolling agreement through 2038, increasing contracted capacity by 35 MW.
  • The Arlington Valley facility now has greater long term revenue visibility tied to peak seasonal electricity demand.
  • Kevin MacIntosh has been appointed as Capital Power's new Chief Financial Officer.

Capital Power, traded on the TSX under the ticker TSX:CPX, is drawing attention with these contract and leadership updates. The shares recently closed at CA$62.37, with a 1 year return of 24.6% and a 5 year return of 104.4%. This provides important context for assessing what these developments may mean for investors following the company.

The extension of Arlington Valley's contract, along with a 35 MW capacity increase, highlights a closer alignment between the asset and peak demand periods. The CFO transition to Kevin MacIntosh is occurring at the same time, so readers may want to observe how capital allocation, financing choices, and risk management evolve as the company continues its commercial optimization and portfolio diversification efforts.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Capital Power by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Capital Power.

2 things going right for Capital Power that this headline doesn't cover.

The Arlington Valley tolling extension through 2038 ties a larger slice of Capital Power’s US gas capacity to long term, contract-backed payments, which can matter for a business that also has exposure to merchant power markets. With roughly 75% of cash flow described as secured through contracts or hedges, this deal fits the company’s focus on visibility. For you as an investor, that can make it easier to think about how contracted earnings stack up against the company’s dividend commitments and interest costs.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Capital Power Narrative

  • The longer-dated Arlington Valley contract supports the narrative that recontracting existing assets and securing long term agreements can help underpin cash flow and support growth plans.
  • The sharp year on year fall in reported Q1 net income to CA$15 million, despite higher sales, highlights the concern in the narrative that margins may be vulnerable if costs, regulation, or competition move against the company.
  • The arrival of a new CFO, after analysts updated their views on margins and earnings power, introduces a leadership element that the existing narrative around execution and capital allocation does not fully address.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Capital Power to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Interest payments are not well covered by earnings, so a period of weaker profitability could put more pressure on the balance sheet.
  • ⚠️ The common share dividend and preference share dividends are not well covered by earnings or free cash flow, which raises questions about how payout levels might interact with future investment needs.
  • 🎁 Earnings are forecast to grow at a double digit rate, which, if achieved, could help ease current pressure points around interest cover and dividend sustainability.
  • 🎁 The Arlington Valley extension adds contracted revenue on top of a diversified portfolio across geographies and technologies, which can help reduce reliance on any single power market or asset.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how the new CFO approaches capital allocation, debt management, and hedging, especially after a quarter where net income moved sharply compared to the prior year. The interaction between long term contracted cash flows, interest costs, and dividend policy will likely remain a key focus, along with how future contract renewals compare to current terms in markets where Capital Power competes with peers such as TransAlta, Emera, or Brookfield Renewable. Execution on integration of recent acquisitions and any further contracting activity around flexible gas and renewable assets will also help show how resilient the earnings mix may be through different power price cycles.

data.title: Galaxy Digital (TSX:GLXY) Q4 Loss Of US$1.04 EPS Tests Bullish Profitability Thesis
data.description:
Galaxy Digital (TSX:GLXY) has just posted another mixed set of numbers, with Q4 2025 revenue of about US$10.2b and a basic EPS loss of US$1.04 per share, rounding out a trailing twelve month period where revenue came in at roughly US$61.4b and basic EPS was a loss of US$0.53. Over recent quarters the company has seen revenue move between about US$8.7b and US$29.2b while basic EPS has swung from a profit of US$3.83 per share in late 2024 to alternating gains and losses through 2025. Investors are likely to focus on how this volatility in earnings power feeds into the margin story now.

See our full analysis for Galaxy Digital.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this pattern of heavy revenue and uneven profitability lines up with the dominant bullish and bearish narratives around Galaxy Digital.

See what the community is saying about Galaxy Digital

Advertisement

Revenue swings from $8.7b to $29.2b in recent quarters

  • Over the last six reported quarters, total revenue has ranged from about US$8.7b in Q3 2024 to US$29.2b in Q3 2025, with Q4 2025 at US$10.2b. This shows that the top line has been large but quite variable.
  • Bulls argue that as Helios and other infrastructure assets move from build out to operation, revenue could become more tied to recurring, long duration cash flows. However, the wide revenue band in the recent history means investors are still waiting for the steadier pattern that the bullish view expects.
    • The bullish narrative points to Helios and tokenization tooling as sources of more fee based revenue. At the same time, the trailing twelve month revenue of about US$61.4b is still coming through with a loss of US$84.9m, so that shift has not yet shown up in the bottom line.
    • Bulls also highlight growing institutional engagement and record client onboarding, but the alternating revenue levels from roughly US$8.7b to US$29.2b suggest that trading and balance sheet activity are still major drivers of results.
Bulls say this is the setup for a powerful earnings reset as Helios ramps, yet the current loss making profile means that thesis is still unproven for now, so it can be worth reading the full bull case before deciding how much weight to give it. 🐂 Galaxy Digital Bull Case

Unprofitable today with widening losses over five years

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Galaxy Digital reported a net income loss of US$84.9m and a basic EPS loss of US$0.53 per share, and over the past five years losses have widened by about 12% per year according to the analysis data.
  • Bears focus on this persistent loss making record and argue that even with forecast earnings growth of about 66.61% per year and expectations of profitability within three years, the path from a US$84.9m loss to positive earnings still depends heavily on execution in areas like Helios, tokenization and venture realizations.
    • Critics highlight that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow, so while losses have averaged about a 12% annual increase, there is less room for error if new projects underperform or if write downs in illiquid holdings continue.
    • The share price has also been described as volatile over the past three months, so the combination of current unprofitability and balance sheet pressure is central to the bearish narrative when questioning how smooth that turnaround can be.
Skeptics point to that widening loss trend and financing pressure as reasons to study the full cautious case on Galaxy before getting comfortable with the risk profile. 🐻 Galaxy Digital Bear Case

Low 0.1x P/S versus peers despite growth forecasts

  • Galaxy Digital trades on a P/S of 0.1x versus a peer average of 2.6x and a US Capital Markets industry average of 3.6x, while analysts are modeling revenue growth of about 27.1% per year and earnings growth of roughly 66.61% per year, with a move to profitability expected within three years.
  • Consensus narrative supporters point out that the analyst price target of US$39.97 is well above the current share price of US$25.42. That view has to be balanced against the fact that the business is still loss making and that the same consensus expects margins only to move from roughly negative to around breakeven in a few years.
    • What stands out is that the price target implies meaningful upside from today while the most recent trailing twelve month figures still show a net loss of US$84.9m, so the gap between current profitability and future expectations is wide.
    • At the same time, the low P/S can be seen as the market demanding proof that the modeled 27.1% revenue growth and return to profitability within three years actually come through in reported numbers.

data.title: Is Masco (MAS) Offering Value After Recent Share Price Pullback And DCF Upside?
data.description:
• Wondering if Masco at around US$71 per share is offering value or just riding sentiment? This article walks through what the current price really reflects.
  • The stock has recently pulled back with a 3.7% decline over the last 7 days, after a strong 21.6% return over 30 days and 19.8% over the past year, which can change how the market is pricing both upside and risk.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Masco as a core player in building products. Investors are watching how housing activity, renovation demand, and input costs could influence future cash flows and pricing power. Alongside that, commentary around interest rate expectations and home improvement trends has been shaping how the market interprets the current share price.
  • Masco currently has a valuation score of 4/6. The sections ahead break down what different valuation approaches suggest about that score, before finishing with a practical way to bring those methods together into a clearer view of value.

Find out why Masco's 19.8% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Masco Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting future cash flows and then discounting them back to today’s value using a required rate of return.

For Masco, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach built on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow stands at about $923.0 million. Analysts provide explicit forecasts for several years, including projected Free Cash Flow of $1,039.0 million in 2029, with further years extrapolated by Simply Wall St to complete a ten year path.

Bringing all of those projected cash flows back to today and summing them produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $87.74 per share. Compared with the current share price around $71, the DCF output suggests Masco trades at roughly an 18.8% discount. On this model alone, the shares appear undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Masco is undervalued by 18.8%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 53 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Masco.

Approach 2: Masco Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Masco, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much you are paying for each dollar of current earnings. This is often where investors look first when comparing established businesses.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher multiple, while lower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower one.

Masco currently trades on a P/E of about 17.18x. That sits below the Building industry average of about 21.51x and well below the broader peer group average of about 62.30x. To refine this, Simply Wall St uses a proprietary “Fair Ratio” that estimates what P/E might make sense for Masco based on factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and risk profile. Because it is tailored to the company rather than broad groups, this Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple comparison with industry or peer averages.

Masco’s Fair Ratio is 23.76x, which is higher than the current 17.18x P/E. This indicates that the shares may be undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

For example, one Masco Narrative on the optimistic end might link a focus on repair and remodel demand, sustainability themes and premium brands to a Fair Value around US$93.13. A more cautious Narrative that emphasizes tariff costs, leadership change and softer DIY markets points to a Fair Value closer to US$69. data.title: Pitney Bowes IL4 Win Puts SendPro 360 And Valuation In Focus
data.description:
• Pitney Bowes (NYSE:PBI) received Provisional Authorization for Impact Level 4 for its SendPro 360 cloud platform.
  • The IL4 status allows use by U.S. Department of Defense agencies and approved defense contractors.
  • The authorization confirms that SendPro 360 meets Department of Defense cybersecurity and data protection requirements.

Pitney Bowes, best known for mailing and shipping solutions, has been pushing further into cloud based platforms, with SendPro 360 at the center of that effort. The new IL4 authorization ties this offering directly to U.S. defense workflows, where secure handling of sensitive information is a baseline requirement rather than an optional feature. For investors watching NYSE:PBI, this connects the company more closely to regulated, higher compliance markets.

Access to Department of Defense agencies and defense contractors broadens the potential customer set for SendPro 360 and may influence how investors view the role of public sector clients in the business mix. Readers will likely focus on how Pitney Bowes pursues active deployments, contract opportunities, and relationships with government buyers following this technical milestone.
